coredata.dc:title: Fruitful fabrication of CDs on GO/g-C3N4 sheets layers: A carbon amalgamation for the remediation of carcinogenic pollutants
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Prakash, K.
Kumar, J. Vinoth
Latha, P.
Kumar, P. Senthil
Karuthapandian, S.
coredata.dc:description:
Herein, for the first time, we developed a novel sheet-on-sheet and metal-free graphene oxide/graphitic carbon nitride/carbon dots (GO/g-C3N4/CDs) ternary nanocomposite was successfully constructed by a novel chemical route in hydrothermal environment and utilized as a visible light photocatalyst for the degradation of carcinogenic Rhodamine B (RhB) and crystal violet (CV) dyes. The crystalline structure and surface morphology of the as-prepared GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite was systematically confirmed by FTIR, XRD, SEM, EDX, TEM, UV-DRS, and XPS techniques. The obtained UV–vis spectroscopy results demonstrate that GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite shows excellent reusable photocatalyst towards the degradation of RhB and CV dyes than pristine g-C3N4 and GO/g-C3N4. The enhanced photocatalytic performances could be ascribed to the balance between the band gap, morphology, crystalline quality and surface area which might be slow down the electron-hole recombination rates. Furthermore, the plausible mechanisms were described from the radical quenching experiments and the results are evident that the
OH radical plays the vital role in the efficient photodegradation process. The superior photocatalytic activity and the reusable ability of the novel GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite could open a new window for the waste water treatment system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst
Metal-free
CDs
g-C3N4
Graphene oxide

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Visible light photocatalytic dye degradation experiments
Probable mechanism for the degradation of dye solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow chart for preparing the GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite photocatalyst.
XRD pattern of g-C3N4, GO, CDs and GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite.
FT-IR spectra of (a) g-C3N4, (b) GO, (c) Carbon dots and (d) GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite.
TEM images of (a) g-C3N4, (b) GO, (c) GO/ g-C3N4, (d) GO/g-C3N4/ CDs ternary nannocompsites.
EDX spectrum of (a) g-C3N4, (b) GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite.
Elemental mapping images of (a) GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite (b) Carbon, (c) Nitrogen and (d) Oxygen.
XPS spectra of GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite (a) Survey spectrum (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s and (d) O 1 s.
Photoluminescence spectra of g-C3N4, GO/ g-C3N4, and GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite.
(a) UV–vis (DRS mode) spectrum of the samples, (b–c) Tauc’s plots of the g-C3N4, GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite and GO/g-C3N4 composite.
Absorption spectra (a) RhB, (b) CV dye solutions irradiated under visible light in the presence of 50 mg of GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanacomposite.
Photodegradation of RhB and CV in the presence of different catalysts.
Effect of initial dye concentration on the photodegradation of (a) RhB and (b) CV dye solution.
Effect of different catalyst weight on the photodegradation of (a) RhB and (b) CV dye solution.
Effect of different pH on the photodegradation of (a) RhB and (b) CV dye solution.
Effect of different scavengers on the photodegradation of (a) RhB and (b) CV dye solution.
(a) XRD pattern of GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite before and after photocatalytic reaction and (b) Reusability of GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Fruitful fabrication of CDs on GO/g-C3N4 sheets layers: A carbon amalgamation for the remediation of carcinogenic pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, VHNSN College, Virudhunagar, 626001, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, 632014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanomaterials Laboratory, IRC, Kalasalingam Academy of Research and Education, Krishnankoil, 626 126, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Kalasalingam Academy of Research and Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Herein, for the first time, we developed a novel sheet-on-sheet and metal-free graphene oxide/graphitic carbon nitride/carbon dots (GO/g-C3N4/CDs) ternary nanocomposite was successfully constructed by a novel chemical route in hydrothermal environment and utilized as a visible light photocatalyst for the degradation of carcinogenic Rhodamine B (RhB) and crystal violet (CV) dyes. The crystalline structure and surface morphology of the as-prepared GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite was systematically confirmed by FTIR, XRD, SEM, EDX, TEM, UV-DRS, and XPS techniques. The obtained UV–vis spectroscopy results demonstrate that GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite shows excellent reusable photocatalyst towards the degradation of RhB and CV dyes than pristine g-C3N4 and GO/g-C3N4. The enhanced photocatalytic performances could be ascribed to the balance between the band gap, morphology, crystalline quality and surface area which might be slow down the electron-hole recombination rates. Furthermore, the plausible mechanisms were described from the radical quenching experiments and the results are evident that the
OH radical plays the vital role in the efficient photodegradation process. The superior photocatalytic activity and the reusable ability of the novel GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite could open a new window for the waste water treatment system.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, the release of industrial waste water into the environment which contains dyes, dyestuffs, pharmaceuticals and hazardous gaseous or liquids are increasing day-to-day. Among them, dyes are the major pollutants which are released through the textile, plastics, pharmaceutical, cosmetic and leather industries. The prolonged release of dyes into the environment could cause carcinogenic and mutagenic to the living things [1–5]. For this reason, the removal of dyes is an important concern to protect the environment. Numerous methods have been used for the waste water treatment including adsorption, coagulation, precipitation, heterogeneous photocatalysis and so forth [6–8]. Among them, heterogeneous photocatalysis technique could offer green and most superior tool in order to degrade hazardous organic dyes into non-toxic inorganic aliphatic acids, CO2 and water [9,10].

In recent years, polymeric graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) have become some of the utmost existing sustainable material, which possesses excellent thermal and chemical stability, small surface area, high electron-hole charge separation rate and the poor photocatalytic activity [11,12]. Several literatures reported that the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 [13], TiO2/g-C3N4 [14], ZnO/g-C3N4 [15] NiMoO4/g-C3N4 [16], CdS/g-C3N4 [17], Ag/g-C3N4 [18], and SiC/g-C3N4 [19], etc. However, the metal-free g-C3N4 could possess a higher recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs which drastically decreases the photocatalytic activity. Various strategies have been adopted to enhance its photocatalytic efficiency which mainly involves metal or non-metal doping [20,21], phosphates [22], metal chalcogenides [23], combining with heterogeneous semiconductors [24,25] and improvement in the photocatalyst surface area [26,27]. These heterostructures have been fabricated extensively to enhance the photocatalytic activity.

The carbon-based materials have been attracted more interested in current years. In particular, carbon nanodots (CDs) have attracted much attention due to it’s excellent physicochemical, mechanical and electronic properties such as high chemical stability, better dissolubility and good electronic conductivity which exhibits excellent electron-acceptor/transport material [28,29]. On the other hand, graphene oxide (GO) is a two-dimensional π conjugation sheet of carbon atoms with a honeycomb structure has attracted much attention due to its peculiar properties including good absorptivity, high surface area, flexible modality structure, high thermal conductivity, mechanical and thermal stabilities, and marvelous charge transfer rate [30–32]. However, it has been utilized as a most versatile material with the preparation of inorganic nanocomposites and has been applied for many potential applications such as drug delivery [33], biochemical sensor [34], electrochemical [35], optoelectronics [36] and catalysis [37] and so on. The electronic integration of g-C3N4 with several carbonaceous materials that have unpaired electrons in the lattice and conjugated including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon black, graphene and carbon nanodots can significantly improve the delocalization, hinder the recombination rate of charge carries and stabilize the charge separation leading to the superior photocatalytic activity [38]. Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, there are no results available on the fabrication of GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposites and its application for photodegradation of RhB and CV dyes.

With this mind, we made an attempt for the synthesis of GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite via simple hydrothermal and sonication treatment. The synthesized g-C3N4, GO and GO/C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite were systematically investigated by various spectroscopic and analytical tools. The SEM and TEM studies were demonstrated that the successful decoration of g-C3N4 sheets and CDs are uniformly decorated on the GO surface. Synergistic effect of the nanocomposite, enhanced photocatalytic performance and stability of photocatalyst was discussed in deeply. Moreover, as-prepared GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite showed superior photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of RhB and CV dyes. The enhanced photocatalytic activity and stability of GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite could be attributed to the good electron reservoir role of CDs and GO which greatly shuttling the photogenerated electrons and holes, and also prevents the aggregation of g-C3N4. The trapping experiment results revealed that •OH radicals are the main reactive species in the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, the reusability and the possible mechanism were also been investigated.

Melamine, graphite powder, potassium permanganate (KMnO4), glucose and all other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

The pristine graphitic-C3N4 (g-C3N4) was synthesized by directly heating melamine. Typically, the melamine precursor was put in an alumina crucible and annealed in an air atmosphere at 550 °C for 4 h at a heating rate of 8 °C min−1. After cooling down to the room temperature, the resulting yellow colored g-C3N4 products were collected and ground into powders.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized using the modified Hummers method [39]. Typically, 1.562 g of graphite powder was dispersed into 23 mL of concentrated H2SO4 (95%) solution in an ice bath. Then, 3 g of KMnO4 was slowly added to the above suspension and ultrasonicated for 12 h at room temperature. Consequently, 150 mL of 30% H2O2 and 46 mL of deionized water drop by drop under sonication. Afterward, the intermediate product was washed with 100 ml of 5% HCl and ethanol several times. Finally, the intermediate product was dried at 65 °C under vacuum for 12 h.

For the synthesis of C/g-C3N4 composite was synthesized by the previously reported article with slight modification [40]. 1.562 g of g-C3N4 and 0.523 g glucose was dissolved to 35 mL of deionized water under vigorous stirring for 2 h. The suspension was then transferred to an 80 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in a drying oven at 185 °C for about 4 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the suspension was washed with distilled water and ethanol and dried at 90 °C for about 12 h in a hot air oven. The pristine CDs were prepared through the similar procedure without the addition of g-C3N4.

The ternary GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite was prepared by ultrasonic treatment as follows: 0.5 g of g-C3N4/CDs and 0.02 g of GO were added into 50 mL of water and then, the suspension was ultrasonicated for 45 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. Afterward, the mixture was centrifuged and washed with water and ethanol, then dried hot air oven at 60 °C for 12 h. Finally, GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposites were obtained which was schematically represented in Scheme 1 .

The surface morphology of the GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposites was studied by a scanning electron microscope with a (model SUPRA 40 SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray analysis spectroscopy (EDX) were obtained on FEI-quanta 200 with an acceleration voltage of 20 kV. The TEM images were carried out PHILIPS CM 200 microscope (accelerating voltage = 200 kV). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was performed using a Rigaku D/MAX 24,000 X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.154178 nm) at the scanning rate of 0.04°/s in the 2θ range from 10 to 80°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was probed by ESCALABMK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded by a Scinco-4100 apparatus. The Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using Shimadzu FT-IR 8400. The UV–vis spectra for the degradation reaction were studied using a Shimadzu 1800 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were deliberate using an (Action research, Spectra Pro 215i PL spectrophotometer) fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visible light photocatalytic dye degradation experiments
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of the as-synthesized GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposites were examined by the degradation of RhB and CV pollutants under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). In a typical procedure, 50 mg of photocatalyst was added to 80 mL of RhB and CV aqueous solution (10 ppm) in a 100 mL reaction vessel. At specific time intervals, 3 mL of aliquots were collected and centrifuged to remove the catalyst particles. The clear supernatant solutions were subjected to measurements of characteristic absorption peaks at 554 and 582 nm using UV–vis spectrophotometer for monitoring the photocatalytic dye degradation. After the degradation experiments, the catalyst was separated from the reaction mixture and then dried, and used to next cycle.

The direct heating of melamine at 500 °C leads to the polymerization of triazine ring to form the polymeric g-C3N4 (Scheme 1(a)). It was well known that the g-C3N4 obviously observed as a layer structure. Moreover, the synthesis of C/g-C3N4 was carried out by the hydrothermal treatment of glucose and g-C3N4 at 180 °C for 8 h. Upon heating, the glucose was completely decomposed and losses its structure which resulted in the formation of CDs. The as-formed CDs were deposited on the layers of the g-C3N4 (Scheme 1(b)). The GO/g-C3N4/CDs was formed by the sonication of as-synthesized GO and g-C3N4/CDs using ultrasonicator for about 45 min. The sonication of GO and g-C3N4/CDs which distributed the particles throughout the medium and resulted in the uniform deposition of GO onto the surface of the g-C3N4/CDs (Scheme 1(c)).

The phase and crystal structure of the as-synthesized g-C3N4, GO and GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposites were probed by XRD studies which are shown in Fig. 1 . From the Fig. 1, the diffraction patterns of g-C3N4 peaks at 2θ = 12.98 and 27.5° could be assigned to (100) and (002) planes of hexagonal phase, which correspond to the interplanar stacking and interlayer stacking of π-conjugated aromatic rings with corresponding to the interplanar distance of 0.674 and 0.326 nm respectively. The obtained results are good consistency with the standard JCPDS File No. 87–1526 [41,42]. The diffraction peak located at 2θ = 11.4° was broader which correspond to the (001) lattice plane (d = 0.83 nm) of amorphous carbon present in GO [43,44]. The broad and weak characteristic peak at 2θ = 26.4° was attributed to the (002) reflection plane of graphitic carbon with an interlayer spacing of 3.36 nm [JCPDS card no 41 -1487]. As observed from the Fig. 1, the appearance of CDs, GO and g-C3N4 related diffraction peaks in the CDs/GO/g-C3N4 nanocomposites, implying that the successful formation of nanocomposites. The average particle size was calculated by using Debye-Scherer’s formula [ 45]
(1) d = 0.9λ/β cos θ
Where, d is the crystallite size, λ is X-ray wavelength, β is (Full Width at Half Maximum) FWHM of the diffraction peak, θ is diffraction angle. The calculated average crystalline sizes were 70.12 and 34.6 nm for the g-C3N4 and GO/g-C3N4/CDs, respectively.

FT-IR spectra of the as-synthesized g-C3N4, GO, CDs, g-C3N4/GO, CDs/g-C3N4, CDs/GO and GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposites were presented in Fig.2 . FT-IR spectra of pure g-C3N4 (curve a) peaks appeared in the range of 1200-1650 cm−1 were assigned to the typical C–N stretching mode of heterocycles [46]. The absorption band at 1645 cm−1 are attributed to CN stretching vibration mode, which established to the sp2 hybridized network in the g-C3N4 network. The peaks at 1240, 1351, 1417 and 1578 cm−1 ascribed to the aromatic C–N stretching [47,48]. Accordingly, the band at 811 cm−1 to the feature stretching vibration of characteristic of s-triazine rings [49]. The broad absorption band of g-C3N4 centered at 3176 cm−1 can be ascribed to the typical stretching vibration modes of terminal NH2 or NH bonds [50]. For the FTIR spectra of GO displays (curve b) the peaks at 1039, 1220 and 1727 cm-1 were associated to stretching vibration of (CO), phenolic (COH), and carbonyl or carboxyl CO groups respectively [51–53]. The peak at 1624 cm-1 is the skeletal vibrations of non-oxidized graphitic domains. FT-IR spectra of pure CDs (curve c) demonstrate the distinctive peaks at 672, 1125, 1635, 2924 and 3450 cm−1 were corresponded to the = C–H, C–O, CO, C–H and OH stretching vibrations, respectively [54]. From the curve d, the existence of g-C3N4, GO and CDs peaks clearly confirm the successful formation of GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite.

The surface morphology, microstructure and particle size of the as-synthesized g-C3N4, GO, GO/C3N4 and GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposites were further evidenced by TEM analysis which is shown in Fig. 3 . The TEM image of pure g-C3N4 shows wrinkled 2D sheet-like morphology (Fig.3a). The selected area diffraction (SAED) pattern of g-C3N4 crystallites (inset Fig. 3a) could be indexed to the (100) and (002) orientation of the hexagonal structure. Fig. 3b represents the thin sheet-like morphology and smooth surfaces of synthesized GO samples. As indicated the Fig.3c, lattice fringes of GO/g-C3N4 was found to be d-spacing of 0.325 nm corresponding to the (002) crystallographic plane of g-C3N4, which further confirmed the GO was deposited on the g-C3N4 surface. The Fig. 3d clearly displays the CDs are well anchoring on the GO and g-C3N4 sheet surfaces with an average diameter 5–30 nm. The TEM studies clearly suggested that successful formation of GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite.

Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was used to determine the elemental composition of the as-synthesized g-C3N4 and GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite and depicted in Fig. 4 a. The EDX spectrum of g-C3N4 demonstrates the appearance of C and N elements. In the EDX spectrum of GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite displays the existence of C, N and O elements without any other impurities. Furthermore, the elemental mapping analysis in Fig. 5 (a–d) revealed that the uniform distribution of the C, N and O elements in the GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out to determine the surface chemical compositions and their electronic states of the as-prepared GO/g-C3N4/CDs and the results are presented in Fig. 6 . The XPS survey spectrum of (Fig.6a) GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite portrays the existence of C1 s, N1 s and oxygen O 1 s elements which good accordance with the EDX report. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum in Fig.6b showed the peaks at the binding energies of 286.4, 287.5 and 288.8 eV were attributable to the carbonyl carbon (CC), hydroxyl carbon (C–O) and carboxyl carbon (OCO), respectively [55]. The peak centered at the binding energy of 399.5 eV were correspond to the sp2 hybridized aromatic N bonded to carbon atoms (CNC) in the triazine rings of g-C3N4 (Fig. 6c) [56]. From the high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectrum in Fig. 6d displayed the three de-convoluted peaks at 530.7, 531.5 and 532.8 eV were ascribed to the lattice, adsorbed and chemisorbed oxygen molecules in the GO/g-C3N4/CDs surface [57]. The XPS results clearly confirm that the existence of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s which suggest that the successful formation of the metal-free GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectrum is often employed to study the separation, transfer, and migration of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the pure g-C3N4, GO/g-C3N4 and GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite which is shown in Fig. 7 . In general, a lower PL intensity expresses a longer lifetime and higher transfer efficiency of the photoinduced charge carries [58–60]. The PL spectra of g-C3N4, GO/g-C3N4 and GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite deliberated an emission peak with center at 438 nm. The PL intensity of GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite and GO/g-C3N4 are much lower than that of pristine g-C3N4, which may be due to the GO and carbon dots are successfully immobilized on the g-C3N4 matrix. It is obvious that GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite has higher migration efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, foremost to enhance the photocatalytic activity.

The optical properties of the different photocatalyst and band gap energy were very important parameter for the selection of the right kind of light needed for the photodegradation of dyes. The absorption spectrum of g-C3N4, GO/g-C3N4, C/g-C3N4, GO/C and GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposites are shown in Fig.8 a. The absorption edge of pristine g-C3N4 was located in the region of 464 nm. The UV visible (DRS mode) absorption spectrum of g-C3N4/GO composite was red shifted significantly due to the support of g-C3N4 sheet. The absorption result shows g-C3N4/CDs and GO/CDs at about 519 and 447 nm respectively. For the GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposites are also demonstrate the strong absorption peak (682 nm) in the visible light region and slightly red-shift of the absorption spectrum was observed. Therefore, the results of GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposites enhance strong light harvesting in the visible region. The energy gap value was calculated using Tauc’s equation and the corresponding Tauc’s plot is shown in Fig.8b and c. The calculated band gap value for the prepared pure g-C3N4, GO/g-C3N4 and GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite was 2.7 and 2.6 and 2.57 eV respectively. The band gap value of GO/g-C3N4/CDs composite was moderately lower when compared to the pure g-C3N4 nanosheets.

The photocatalytic efficiency of the GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite was evaluated by the degradation of RhB and CV dye solutions under visible light irradiation. The progress of photodegradation of RhB and CV was monitored by UV–vis spectrum which is shown in Fig. 9 (a–b). From Fig. 9(a–b) shows that time-dependent UV–vis absorption spectrum of RhB and CV dye solution in the presence of 50 mg GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite under visible light illumination. It obvious that the main characteristic peak of RhB was observed at 554 nm and CV at 587 nm gradually decreases with the increasing of illumination time and 99% dye degradation efficiency was achieved within 35 and 60 min for RhB and CV dyes respectively. On the other hand, pure g-C3N4 and GO/g-C3N4 shows only 29% and 41% degradation of RhB and 33% and 53% degradation of CV dyes after 35 and 60 min respectively which is shown in Fig.10 (a–b). Fig.10(a–b) shows that there was no significant degradation was observed in the absence of catalyst as well as in the dark condition. The enhanced photocatalytic performance is due to the synergistic effect of CDs between the GO and g-C3N4 which may slow down the photogenerated charge carriers.

The effect of initial concentration of RhB and CV dye solution on the photocatalytic degradation process over GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite was tested by varying dye concentration 5–20 ppm under the identical reaction conditions and the results are presented in Fig. 11 . It was noticed that the rate of photodegradation increased with increasing the concentration of the dye solution up to 10 ppm. After that, degradation efficiency was decreased which might be due to the non-availability of oxidative free radicals ions (O2
− and
OH) at higher dye concentrations and the larger amount of organic dye molecules competing for the effect of photodegradation route.

Fig.12 shows the effect of GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite photocatalyst dosage was also investigated by varying the catalyst weight from 10 to 75 mg. It observed that 50 mg of photocatalyst loading has the higher efficiency, beyond that the degradation efficiency was maintained at constant. The main reason is due to the higher amount of catalyst restrict the active species absorbed on the photocatalyst surface. Therefore, 50 mg of the photocatalyst is much enough for the effective photodegradation of RhB and CV dyes.

The degradation efficiency of the RhB and CV dye solution were evaluated as a function of different pH and the results are shown in Fig. 13 . The influence of pH on the photodegradation of dye molecules was investigated in the pH ranges from 2–10. The results indicated that higher photodegradation efficiency occurs at pH 10 than other pH ranges. Since the photocatalytic efficiency was low in an acidic medium which is due to the surface of nanoparticle are became increasingly with strong electrostatic repulsion with the dye solution. Accordingly, the increasing pH of the dye solution, the surface hydroxyl radicals (•OH) are accountable for the degradation of dye solution which improved the photocatalytic performance [61–63].

The heterogeneous photocatalytic reactions mainly take place by the active involvement of ROS species such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radical anions (O2 •−). To identify the active ROS involvement, the photodegradation reaction was carried out in the presence of various scavengers viz triphenylphosphine (TPP), acryl amide (AA) and azide ion (AZ) for O2
− and benzoic acid (BA) for
OH radicals. Fig. 14 (a & b) portrays that the addition of AZ, TPP and AA into the reaction suspension were slightly retarded the photodegradation efficiency [64–67]. In contrarily, the introduction of BA was greatly restricts the RhB and CV degradation efficiency [68,69]. Hence, the results concluded that the •OH radicals participate the main role and O2
− plays the minor role for the photodegradation of RhB and CV dye solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probable mechanism for the degradation of dye solution
ce:section.ce:para:

• O H

m i n e r a l i z e d
p r o d u c t s
The light illumination on the GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite was led to the excitation of an electron from the VB of g-C3N4 to the CB which leaves a hole in the VB as shown in Eq. (2). The electron in the CB was possibly followed the two cases, in the first case the electron may be trapped and conducted by the GO as it generally a good conductor of electrons and in the second case some of the electrons may be reacted with a water molecule to produce the active •OH radical. Moreover, the conducted electrons may be also participated in the reaction and produce an
OH radical upon reaction with a water molecule (Eqs. 3). The holes in the VB were reacted with the atmospheric oxygen in the system and generated a superoxide radical anion (Eq. 4). Finally, the organic dye solutions could be largely degraded by
OH radicals (Eq. 5) during the photocatalytic process under visible light irradiations and generate the mineralized products like CO2, H2O, etc (Eq. 6). The role of CD enhances the rate of absorption of photons by providing the large surface and acts as a very solid support to the GO and g-C3N4.

To confirm the stability and reusability of GO/g-C3N4/CDs, the catalyst was characterized using XRD before and after photocatalytic reaction which was displayed in Fig.15 . The synthesized GO/g-C3N4/CDs photocatalyst were carried out by several recycle experiments. The XRD pattern of used photocatalyst indicated that the intensity of characteristic peaks of composites was not significantly decreased which is shown in Fig. 15a. The Fig. 15b shows the photodegradation of RhB for first three cycle’s value sustained at 90% and after that photocatalytic efficiency was gradually decreased little bit and maintained 85% to 5th cycle. The above results suggested that GO/g-C3N4/CDs photocatalyst is a stable and reusability against the organic pollutant dye degradation technique under the visible light irradiation.

In summary, we have developed a novel metal-free GO/g-C3N4/CDs ternary nanocomposite was successfully synthesized via hydrothermal environment followed by a simple sonication techniques. The synthesized GO/g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite was characterized in detailed. The morphological analysis showed that the CDs/g-C3N4 is well intruded in the GO matrix. The photocatalysis result showed that ternary GO/g-C3N4/CDs photocatalyst exhibited an excellent photocatalytic efficiency towards the degradation of RhB and CV dyes. The PL spectra results revealed that the enhanced photocatalytic performance which is attributed to the efficient charge separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Besides, the active species trapping experiment by different scavengers revealed that the photocatalytic activity mainly attributed by superoxide radicals. This work may provide new possibilities for the improvement of a high effective metal-free photocatalyst for the degradation of organic contaminants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their sincere thanks to the College managing board, Principal and Head of the Department, VHNSN College for providing necessary research facilities.
